questions.stem: Two continuous maps $f, g : X \to Y$ are homotopic when there exists a continuous map $H : X \times [0,1] \to Y$ such that:
options: $f$ and $g$ agree on a dense set.
questions.explanation: A homotopy from $f$ to $g$ is a continuous family $H$ interpolating between them: $H(\cdot, 0) = f$ and $H(\cdot, 1) = g$. The parameter $t$ is thought of as time deforming $f$ into $g$.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A path in a space $X$ is:
options: any subset of $X$ homeomorphic to a line.
questions.explanation: A path is a continuous function from the unit interval $[0,1]$ into $X$. Its endpoints are $\gamma(0)$ and $\gamma(1)$; the path itself is the map, not just its image.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A path homotopy between paths $\gamma_0, \gamma_1 : [0,1] \to X$ is a homotopy $H$ that additionally:
options: keeps the endpoints fixed: $H(0,t)$ and $H(1,t)$ are constant in $t$.
questions.explanation: A path homotopy fixes endpoints throughout: $H(0, t) = \gamma_0(0)$ and $H(1, t) = \gamma_0(1)$ for all $t$. This is the relevant equivalence for paths with common endpoints.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Given paths $\gamma$ from $x_0$ to $x_1$ and $\delta$ from $x_1$ to $x_2$, the concatenation $\gamma \cdot \delta$ is the path that:
options: is the pointwise sum of $\gamma$ and $\delta$.
questions.explanation: The product path $\gamma \cdot \delta$ does $\gamma$ at double speed on the first half of the interval and $\delta$ at double speed on the second half. It is defined exactly when the end of $\gamma$ equals the start of $\delta$.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The reverse $\bar{\gamma}$ of a path $\gamma$ is defined by:
questions.explanation: The reverse path retraces $\gamma$ backwards: $\bar{\gamma}(s) = \gamma(1-s)$. It runs from $\gamma(1)$ to $\gamma(0)$ and serves as the inverse up to path homotopy.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Homotopy of maps $X \to Y$ (relative to nothing) is:
options: an equivalence relation on the set of continuous maps $X \to Y$.
questions.explanation: Homotopy is reflexive (the constant homotopy), symmetric (reverse the time parameter), and transitive (concatenate homotopies in the time variable). Hence it is an equivalence relation, and one writes $f \simeq g$.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A space $X$ is contractible when:
options: the identity map $\mathrm{id}_X$ is homotopic to a constant map.
questions.explanation: Contractibility means $\mathrm{id}_X \simeq c$ for some constant map $c$. Equivalently, $X$ is homotopy equivalent to a point. Such a homotopy is called a contraction.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Which space is contractible?
questions.explanation: A convex set $C$ is contractible: the straight-line homotopy $H(x,t) = (1-t)x + t x_0$ to any fixed point $x_0 \in C$ stays in $C$ by convexity. The circle, torus, and figure-eight all have nontrivial fundamental group and are not contractible.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A homotopy equivalence between $X$ and $Y$ is a map $f : X \to Y$ for which there exists $g : Y \to X$ with:
questions.explanation: A homotopy equivalence requires a map $g$ such that the composites are homotopic (not equal) to the respective identities. Equality of composites would be the stronger condition of being homeomorphisms.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Up to path homotopy, concatenating a path $\gamma$ with its reverse, $\gamma \cdot \bar{\gamma}$, gives:
options: a path with no fixed endpoints.
questions.explanation: The product $\gamma \cdot \bar{\gamma}$ is path-homotopic to the constant path at the starting point $\gamma(0)$. This is exactly the statement that reversal provides inverses in the fundamental group.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Concatenation of paths, up to path homotopy, is associative. This means:
options: only two paths may ever be concatenated.
questions.explanation: The two parenthesizations are not equal as functions (they reparametrize differently), but they are path-homotopic via a reparametrization homotopy. This is why associativity holds at the level of homotopy classes.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A deformation retraction of $X$ onto a subspace $A$ is a homotopy $H : X \times [0,1] \to X$ with $H(\cdot, 0) = \mathrm{id}_X$, $H(X \times \{1\}) = A$, and:
options: $H(x, t) = x$ for all $x \in X$, all $t$.
$H(a, t) = a$ for all $a \in A$, all $t$.
questions.explanation: A deformation retraction continuously pushes $X$ onto $A$ while keeping every point of $A$ fixed throughout: $H(a,t) = a$ for $a \in A$. If $X$ deformation retracts onto $A$, then $A \hookrightarrow X$ is a homotopy equivalence.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The annulus (cylinder) $S^1 \times [0,1]$ is homotopy equivalent to:
questions.explanation: The cylinder deformation retracts onto a core circle $S^1 \times \{1/2\}$, so it is homotopy equivalent to $S^1$. Homotopy equivalence is weaker than homeomorphism: the cylinder is not homeomorphic to a circle.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The punctured plane $\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{0\}$ is homotopy equivalent to:
questions.explanation: Radial deformation retraction $x \mapsto x / |x|$ collapses $\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{0\}$ onto the unit circle, so it is homotopy equivalent to $S^1$. In particular it is not contractible.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: If $f \simeq f'$ and $g \simeq g'$ (with composites defined), then:
options: nothing can be said about $g \circ f$.
questions.explanation: Homotopy is compatible with composition: homotopic maps compose to homotopic maps, so $g \circ f \simeq g' \circ f'$. This is what makes the homotopy category well-defined.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Two maps into a convex set $C \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ are:
options: always homotopic, via the straight-line homotopy.
homotopic only if they are equal.
homotopic only if both are constant.
questions.explanation: Any two maps $f, g : X \to C$ into a convex set are homotopic by the straight-line homotopy $H(x,t) = (1-t)f(x) + t g(x)$, which stays in $C$ by convexity. Hence $C$ has the homotopy type of a point for mapping purposes.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A path-connected space is one in which:
options: the only clopen sets are trivial.
any two points are joined by a path.
every open cover has a finite subcover.
questions.explanation: Path-connectedness means for any $x_0, x_1 \in X$ there is a path $\gamma : [0,1] \to X$ with $\gamma(0) = x_0$ and $\gamma(1) = x_1$. This implies connectedness but is strictly stronger.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Homotopy equivalence of spaces is:
options: a strictly weaker relation that is not symmetric.
an equivalence relation on topological spaces.
questions.explanation: Homotopy equivalence is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive, so it partitions spaces into homotopy types. It is coarser than homeomorphism: a disk and a point are homotopy equivalent but not homeomorphic.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The constant path $c_{x_0}$ at $x_0$ acts as an identity for concatenation up to path homotopy, meaning:
questions.explanation: Pre- or post-composing a path with the appropriate constant path yields a path homotopic to the original (after reparametrization). So constant paths are two-sided identities at the level of homotopy classes.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Which statement is TRUE?
options: Homotopy equivalent spaces have isomorphic fundamental groups (for path-connected spaces).
Every contractible space is homeomorphic to a point.
Homotopic maps must be equal on a dense set.
A homotopy equivalence is always a bijection.
questions.explanation: Homotopy equivalent path-connected spaces have isomorphic fundamental groups, since $\pi_1$ is a homotopy invariant. Contractible spaces need not be homeomorphic to a point (for example $\mathbb{R}^n$), and homotopy equivalences need not be bijections.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: A retraction of $X$ onto a subspace $A$ is a continuous map $r : X \to A$ such that:
options: $r$ restricted to $A$ is the identity, i.e. $r(a) = a$ for all $a \in A$.
questions.explanation: A retraction fixes $A$ pointwise: $r|_A = \mathrm{id}_A$. A deformation retraction is a retraction that is moreover homotopic to $\mathrm{id}_X$ through maps fixing $A$.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: If $X$ is contractible, then any two maps $f, g : Y \to X$ are:
questions.explanation: Into a contractible target, all maps are homotopic to a constant, hence to each other. So $[Y, X]$ has a single element whenever $X$ is contractible.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: The letters of the alphabet, viewed as subsets of the plane, are sorted by homotopy type. Which pair is homotopy equivalent?
questions.explanation: $\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathrm{L}$ are each homeomorphic to an interval, hence contractible and homotopy equivalent. $\mathrm{O}$ has one loop while $\mathrm{X}$ is contractible; $\mathrm{A}$ has one loop while $\mathrm{B}$ has two.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Reparametrizing a path $\gamma$ by a continuous nondecreasing surjection $\varphi : [0,1] \to [0,1]$ with $\varphi(0)=0$, $\varphi(1)=1$ yields a path $\gamma \circ \varphi$ that is:
options: a different homotopy class in general.
questions.explanation: Reparametrization by such a $\varphi$ does not change the path homotopy class: $\gamma \circ \varphi \simeq \gamma$ rel endpoints. This fact underlies why concatenation is well-defined on homotopy classes.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Classify simple spaces by whether they are contractible.
questions.solution: A convex subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$, such as the closed disk $D^2$, {{1}} contractible. The circle $S^1$ {{2}} contractible, since it has a nontrivial loop. Counting how many of the three spaces $D^2$, a single point, and $S^1$ are contractible gives {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The disk (convex) and a point are contractible; the circle is not. So $2$ of the three are contractible.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Identify what familiar space each example is homotopy equivalent to.
questions.solution: The punctured plane $\mathbb{R}^2 \setminus \{0\}$ deformation retracts onto a circle, so up to homotopy it is {{1}}. The solid cylinder $S^1 \times [0,1]$ also retracts onto its core circle, so it is homotopy equivalent to {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Both the punctured plane and the cylinder deformation retract onto a circle, so each is homotopy equivalent to $S^1$.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Track endpoints under reversal and concatenation of paths.
questions.solution: Let $\gamma$ be a path with $\gamma(0) = a$ and $\gamma(1) = b$. Its reverse $\bar{\gamma}$ goes from $b$ back to $a$. The concatenation $\gamma \cdot \bar{\gamma}$ is a loop, and up to path homotopy it equals the constant path; the number of distinct endpoints of this loop is {{1}}. Whether $\gamma \cdot \bar{\gamma}$ is null-homotopic rel endpoints: answer {{2}}.
questions.explanation: A loop based at $a$ has a single endpoint (start equals end), so the count is $1$. And $\gamma \cdot \bar{\gamma}$ is path-homotopic to the constant loop, so it is null-homotopic: yes.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology

questions.stem: Use the algebraic properties of path concatenation up to homotopy.
questions.solution: On homotopy classes of paths, concatenation is associative, has constant paths as identities, and reversal gives inverses. The constant path $c_a$ at a point $a$ serves as a {{1}} for that product. The number of two-sided identity classes based at a fixed point $a$ (the constant loop) is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The constant path class is the unique two-sided identity for path concatenation at a basepoint, so there is exactly $1$ such identity.
questions.source: in the style of Hatcher, Algebraic Topology
